specification.topic-statement: The REPL tool execution system runs tool calls requested by the REPL (synchronously) or by the LLM (asynchronously on a worker thread), persisting both the tool call and its result to the conversation history and scrollback.

scope.in-scope: Synchronous REPL-driven tool execution, asynchronous LLM-driven tool execution (thread start, worker logic, and completion harvest), thinking block preservation in tool call messages, the on-complete hook lifecycle, and thread state cleanup.
scope.boundary: How the tool registry is consulted, how an external tool is forked and executed, and how the raw result is wrapped in a standard envelope are handled by the tool execution and tool wrapper subsystems and are out of scope here.
The transitions between idle, waiting-for-LLM, and executing-tool states are handled by the agent state machine and are out of scope here.
How the LLM streaming response is parsed to detect a tool call and how thinking blocks are accumulated during streaming are out of scope here.
How the conversation is resubmitted to the LLM after a tool result is recorded is out of scope here.

field.name: tool name
field.note: deep-copied from pending tool call at thread start; owned by the thread context

field.name: arguments
field.note: deep-copied from pending tool call at thread start; owned by the thread context

field.name: agent reference
field.note: live pointer to the agent context; used by internal tool handlers

field.name: registry reference
field.note: live pointer to the shared tool registry

behavior.synchronous-execution:
When the REPL itself drives a tool call (as opposed to an LLM response), execution is performed synchronously on the main thread without spawning a worker.
The pending tool call is retrieved from the agent. The tool is dispatched directly to the external execution path (no registry lookup for internal tools occurs in this path). The result is returned immediately.
A tool call message is created from the pending call's identifier, name, and arguments, with no thinking blocks. The message is appended to the conversation.
A tool result message is created from the identifier and the result output. It is appended to the conversation.
Both messages are rendered to the scrollback buffer and persisted to the database. The data payload stored alongside the tool call record contains no thinking blocks.
The pending tool call is freed and cleared after persistence.
The synchronous path always creates the tool call message without thinking or redacted-thinking blocks. Thinking accumulation only occurs during LLM streaming, which does not apply to REPL-driven tool calls.
behavior.async-start:
When an LLM response requests a tool call, asynchronous execution is initiated on the current agent.
Before the thread is created, the on-complete hook for the requested tool is looked up in the registry and stored on the agent. If the tool is not found in the registry or the registry is absent, the on-complete hook is set to null.
A thread-owned allocation context is created. Within it, the tool name and arguments are deep-copied from the pending tool call so that the worker thread has stable, immutable access to its inputs for its entire lifetime.
The completion flag is set to false and the running flag is set to true under the mutex before the thread is spawned. The agent is then transitioned to the executing-tool state.
If thread creation fails, the running flag is cleared under the mutex and the application panics.
The hook lookup happens once at thread start, not at completion time. This ensures the hook stored on the agent matches the tool that was actually launched, even if the registry were modified concurrently.
behavior.worker-thread:
The worker thread runs concurrently with the main event loop.
The tool name is looked up in the registry. If an internal tool is found, its handler is called directly with the thread-owned allocation context, the agent context, and the argument string. The handler must return a non-null JSON result string; if it returns null, a failure envelope is produced instead.
If no internal tool is found, the tool name is dispatched to the external execution path, which forks and execs the tool process.
After the result is obtained by either path, it is stored in a shared field on the agent. The completion flag is then set to true under the mutex, signaling the main loop.
The worker thread writes the result pointer directly to a field on the agent context. Ownership of this allocation is transferred to the main thread when the thread is joined during the completion phase.
behavior.async-complete:
Completion is performed on the main thread after the main loop detects that the worker thread's completion flag is set.
The worker thread is joined. The result stored in the shared agent field is moved into a locally owned allocation before any further processing.
A tool call message is created that includes any thinking content accumulated during the LLM streaming phase: the thinking text, the thinking signature, the redacted-thinking data, and the tool-call-specific thought signature are all incorporated into the message. After the message is created, each of these thinking fields on the agent is freed and cleared.
The tool call and tool result messages are persisted to the database before being appended to the in-memory conversation array. Both messages are then rendered to the scrollback buffer.
After persistence and rendering, the pending tool call is freed and cleared.
The thread-owned allocation context is freed and cleared unless an on-complete hook is set. If a hook is set, the thread context is left intact because the hook may reference data allocated within it (such as a newly created child agent object). The hook is responsible for managing the lifetime of that data.
Thread state fields on the agent (running flag, completion flag, result pointer, child process identifier) are all cleared under the mutex. The agent is then transitioned out of the executing-tool state.
The tool call message for an LLM-driven tool includes the full thinking content from the preceding LLM response turn. This preserves the provider's thinking trace in the conversation record so it can be replayed and resubmitted on future turns.
Database persistence of both the tool call and tool result messages occurs before the messages are added to the in-memory conversation array. This ordering ensures the database record exists even if the in-memory append were to fail.
Freeing the thread context while the hook still holds references into it would produce dangling pointers. The thread context survives until after the hook has finished executing.
